prompt:
Write a single paragraph of nocturnal prose describing a fishing town after dark, under ALL of these constraints:
- The letter 'e' (upper or lower case) must NOT appear anywhere in your text. This is a strict lipogram.
- The paragraph must consist of exactly 9 sentences. End sentences only with '.', '!' or '?', and do not use those three characters anywhere else (no abbreviations written with periods, no ellipses, and no digits at all anywhere in the text).
- The first letters of the 9 sentences, in order, must spell NOCTURNAL (sentence 1 begins with capital N, sentence 2 with O, sentence 3 with C, sentence 4 with T, sentence 5 with U, sentence 6 with R, sentence 7 with N, sentence 8 with A, sentence 9 with L). Each sentence must begin directly with its required capital letter: no leading punctuation or quotation marks.
- The paragraph must contain between 110 and 150 words (inclusive; a word is any whitespace-separated token).
- It must include each of these words at least once: fog, salt, midnight, harbor.
- It must include the exact phrase: night is a black harbor
- One paragraph only: no blank lines, no headings, no list items.
Output ONLY the piece itself in plain ASCII text: no title, no preamble, no explanation, no markdown code fences, no trailing commentary.

rubric.question: Is the response a single paragraph (no blank-line breaks)?
rubric.decision_rule: Split on blank lines; true iff exactly 1 block.

rubric.question: Does the paragraph consist of exactly 9 sentences?
rubric.decision_rule: Split on ./!/? terminators; true iff exactly 9 sentences.

rubric.question: Is the letter 'e' (either case) completely absent?
Do the first letters of the 9 sentences spell NOCTURNAL?
rubric.decision_rule: True iff sentence initials are N O C T U R N A L, in order, uppercase.

rubric.question: Does the paragraph contain at least 110 words?
rubric.decision_rule: True iff there are 110 or more whitespace-separated words.

rubric.question: Does the paragraph contain at most 150 words?
rubric.decision_rule: True iff there are 150 or fewer whitespace-separated words.

rubric.question: Does the text include all of: fog, salt, midnight, harbor?
rubric.decision_rule: True iff each word appears at least once as a whole word.

rubric.question: Does the text include the exact phrase 'night is a black harbor'?
rubric.decision_rule: True iff the phrase appears literally (any capitalization).

rubric.question: Are digits absent from the text?
rubric.decision_rule: True iff no digit characters 0-9 appear.

rubric.question: Is the paragraph coherent, grammatical prose that vividly evokes the fishing town at night, rather than a list of disconnected constraint-satisfying words?
rubric.decision_rule: True iff the nine sentences read as one continuous nocturnal scene in natural English.

prompt:
Write a SNOWBALL PARAGRAPH about mapmaking and the limits of maps: a single paragraph of exactly 12 sentences in which sentence 1 contains exactly 1 word, sentence 2 exactly 2 words, sentence 3 exactly 3 words, and so on, adding one word per sentence up to sentence 12 with exactly 12 words (78 words in total). Constraints:
- The first letters of the 12 sentences, in order, must spell CARTOGRAPHER (sentence 1 begins with capital C, sentence 2 with A, sentence 3 with R, sentence 4 with T, sentence 5 with O, sentence 6 with G, sentence 7 with R, sentence 8 with A, sentence 9 with P, sentence 10 with H, sentence 11 with E, sentence 12 with R). Each sentence must begin directly with its required capital letter: no leading punctuation or quotation marks.
- Counting rules: a word is any run of characters containing no spaces and no sentence-ending punctuation; each sentence must end with '.', '!' or '?', and those three characters must not appear anywhere except at the ends of sentences (no abbreviations written with periods, no ellipses). Commas, semicolons, colons, hyphens, and apostrophes inside a sentence are fine and do not split or end a word.
- The word 'cartographer' must appear somewhere in the paragraph.
- The exact phrase 'every map forgets its maker' must appear (any capitalization).
- No digits anywhere.
- One paragraph only: no blank lines.
Output ONLY the piece itself in plain ASCII text: no title, no preamble, no explanation, no markdown code fences, no trailing commentary.

rubric.question: Is the response a single paragraph (no blank-line breaks)?
rubric.decision_rule: Split on blank lines; true iff exactly 1 block.

rubric.question: Does the paragraph consist of exactly 12 sentences?
rubric.decision_rule: Split on ./!/? terminators; true iff exactly 12 sentences.

rubric.question: Does sentence k contain exactly k words for k = 1..12, with the sentence initials spelling CARTOGRAPHER and '.', '!', '?' appearing only as sentence terminators?
rubric.decision_rule: True iff the paragraph is exactly 12 sentences whose word counts are 1,2,...,12 in order, sentence k begins with the k-th capital letter of CARTOGRAPHER, and no sentence-ending punctuation appears inside a sentence.

rubric.question: Does the paragraph contain the word 'cartographer'?
Does the text include the exact phrase 'every map forgets its maker'?
rubric.decision_rule: True iff the phrase appears literally (any capitalization).

rubric.question: Are digits absent from the text?
rubric.decision_rule: True iff no digit characters 0-9 appear.

rubric.question: Does the paragraph read as one coherent meditation on mapmaking and the limits of maps, with each sentence following from the last, rather than twelve disconnected fragments?
rubric.decision_rule: True iff the twelve sentences develop a single connected line of thought about maps and territory in natural English.
